items.title: Shakespeare, Bowie, Einstein: Mona opens one-of-a-kind underground library

children.content: From Shakespeare's First Folio to neon-lit shelves to a curvy kids' section, Mona's new library, Phrontisterion,
is anything but ordinary.

gallery.altText: Book on display with hand reaching for a dial device.
Room with curved seating and roof design.
Shelves filled with books line the walls of a library. At the end of the corridor is a cave-like reading room.

thumbnail.altText: Book on display with hand reaching for a dial device.
Room with curved seating and roof design.
Shelves filled with books line the walls of a library. At the end of the corridor is a cave-like reading room.

items.title: Bird flu could be catastrophic for nation's vulnerable species, experts warn

children.content: The black swan, the Tasmanian devil and Australian sea lions are among the species at risk if a deadly strain of avian influenza takes hold in Australia, experts are warning.

image.altText: Tasmanian Devils will be released into a 1000 acre sanctuary to interact with other native mainland animals.

items.title: How this bus stop that hasn't changed cost a city almost $9m

children.content: Repeated failed attempts to shift this bus stop has cost ratepayers millions over the past 15 years. The council is now trying again, but some warn it is already "a dog's breakfast".

image.altText: A bus arriving at a bus stop, a man with an assistance dog can be seen.

items.title: Victoria stopped native forest logging, only to subsidise it in Tasmania

children.content: Four Corners follows the logs, and the money, to uncover how efforts to end logging have shifted the timber trade across state borders.

gallery.altText: Two yellow excavators with claw attachments sit next to piles of logs. The end of a forest can be seen in the background.

thumbnail.altText: Two yellow excavators with claw attachments sit next to piles of logs. The end of a forest can be seen in the background.

images.altText: Nathan Ngoy is shown a red card

articles.title: How Australia's World Cup chef knows the players like his food

children.content: Chef Vini Capovilla has learnt a thing or two catering for the Socceroos at four World Cups. And one is finding out if they like his food.

image.altText: The Socceroos have a team barista in the hotel

articles.title: How is the most culturally diverse World Cup ever being received in Trump's America?

children.content: Fans from all over the world are being welcomed by locals across North America to celebrate the World Cup. But can it make a lasting difference in the US where President Donald Trump has cracked down on immigration?

image.altText: A group of Haiti football fans cheer inside the stadium

children.content: Lamine Yamal finds the net on his World Cup starting debut, as Spain makes light work of Saudi Arabia to bounce back from its shock draw with Cabo Verde. Follow live.

avatar.alt: A headshot of a man with sandy brown hair and clear plastic glasses smiling at the camera. Blue collared shirt.

items.title: Belgium down to 10 men after dominating Iran in stalemate
Concerns over AI and data centres a dangerous force for Labor

children.content: While the government is trying to assure people there is nothing to fear from AI, many think technology is moving too quickly for authorities to keep up.

image.altText: A man in a vampire outfit lies on the steps of an office building with protest signs against data centres behind him.

items.title: A major change to super payments will start next week

children.content: Many businesses support the payday superannuation changes, but some are concerned they will not be able to meet the obligations.

image.altText: Woman with blonde hair and blue shirt sits at desk looking at camera

items.title: Cocaine bricks wash up on Fiji beach as 'suspicious yachts' sail by

children.content: Locals on Fiji's remote Komo Island found one of the 27 bricks of cocaine ashore in recent weeks, and they're worried about what the find will bring.

gallery.altText: a Fijian man on a beach looking out to sea

thumbnail.altText: a Fijian man on a beach looking out to sea

items.title: Betting apps know more about you than you might think

children.content: Betting ads on TV have been front of mind in Canberra lately, but experts say we're overlooking the real firepower behind Australia's gambling industry: cutting-edge, hyper-personalised, online tech.

image.altText: A photo over the shoulder of a woman as she looks at a gambling app on her phone.

items.title: Think writing off something on tax makes it free? It doesn't

children.content: Advertisers tout EOFY sales as a prime time for big buys, and hint at the promise of a generous tax refund, but that's not quite how it works.

image.altText: A collage of a series of end-of-financial-year sales tickets.

items.title: Colleen Harkin quits Liberal party after praising One Nation

children.content: Liberal Party member and think tank education director Colleen Harkin has quit the party a week after attending a One Nation fundraiser in Melbourne.

image.altText: Colleen Harkin wears a pink blazer

items.title: Auction clearance rates fall to lowest level in six years

children.content: Not since the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic have sellers seen such disappointing results at auction, with Cotality predicting "a further loss of momentum" in the housing market.

image.altText: A man wearing a suit gesticulates at an auction with a house behind

items.title: Preserving lesbian history, one portrait at a time

children.content: When Stacey Bennett came out at 14, she felt alone. Now the Geelong-based artist has painted 100 lesbians to ensure future generations know they are part of a rich and diverse community.

gallery.altText: A woman with curly blonde hair sitting in front of a wall of portraits of women she has painted.

thumbnail.altText: A woman with curly blonde hair sitting in front of a wall of portraits of women she has painted.

items.title: History quiz: Where was Ned Kelly finally captured?

children.content: Can you answer our questions about Anna Wintour, Australian tennis royalty and the doughnuts of the past? Test yourself.

image.altText: A large statue of Ned Kelly in his armour holding a gun

items.title: How many clothes should you own? The maths behind a sustainable wardrobe

children.content: The environmental impact of a garment often depends on how many times it's worn. In a sense, every additional wear helps "offset" the garment's carbon footprint.

image.altText: A woman with a surprised expression pokes her head out of a pile of brightly coloured clothes

items.title: Scott Miller thinks Australia probably hates him. He's desperate to reverse the damage

children.content: From Olympic silver medallist to convicted drug supplier, Scott Miller opens up on one of the biggest downfalls in the history of Australian sport and the moments that saved his life as he seeks redemption.

items.title: On health and child care, Labor might have found Pauline Hanson's kryptonite

children.content: Pauline Hanson said thousands of words in her marathon appearance at the National Press Club, but only a few of them will be heard widely.

image.altText: Photos of Pauline Hanson and Anthony Albanese

items.title: Opposition backs Israel's right to military action in Lebanon

children.content: The Shadow Foreign Affairs Minister has defended Israel’s right to continue its military operation in Lebanon, despite an MOU between the US and Iran which commits to an end to the war.

image.altText: Ted O’Brien at a press conference.

items.title: The UK's version of One Nation just got smashed. Could our PM learn from it?

children.content: Britain's unpopular Labour government just scored a thumping by-election victory, but what could look like a blueprint for combating a populist opponent could actually be a warning for Anthony Albanese.

gallery.altText: A man in a suit and glasses sits still, concentrating on something in the distance.

thumbnail.altText: A man in a suit and glasses sits still, concentrating on something in the distance.

avatar.alt: A man smiling at the camera.

items.title: Why fuel excise cut is being extended by another month but at smaller discount

children.content: The fuel excise cut was due to stop at the end of the month but will continue to a lesser extent until August. Here's why.

image.altText: Two hands on a premium petrol pump.

items.title: Inside the decades-long hunt for a Long Island serial killer

children.content: Long Island's Ocean Parkway was – for at least one killer – the perfect place to leave a body. With Rex Heuermann behind bars, some questions remain unanswered.

image.altText: a man on a background of a beach and a street sign saying GIlgo Beach

items.title: Trump says Reflecting Pool may be drained for repairs after 'vandalism'

children.content: Donald Trump says the newly revamped Reflecting Pool needs "repairs" and that multiple people have been arrested for vandalising the Washington DC water feature.

image.altText: An aerial view of the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool showing large patches of green

items.title: WA football great Cousins taking inspiration from Daniher in MND fight

children.content: WA football great Bryan Cousins is taking inspiration from the late Neale Daniher in his fight with motor neurone disease, as he pays tribute to son Ben for his efforts in combating addiction.

image.altText: Close-up of a man with short grey hair

items.title: Bird flu detection 'a distant concern' for Victoria, industry says

children.content: The head of Victoria's peak farming group says the detection of the deadly H5 variant of avian influenza in Western Australia is a "distant concern" for Victorians.

image.altText: Dozens of hens with glossy brown feathers and red plump combs under a clear blue sky.

items.title: They bought into Australia's tallest tower. Now their deposits are in limbo

children.content: Apartment buyers who were sold on ambitious plans for the tallest building in the country say they're facing up to a decade of uncertainty after the head-turning project faltered.

image.altText: a mock up image of what the tower would look like when complete.

items.title: Love story: Coming out as a throuple

children.content: Everyone is bisexual, polyamorous, and has a crush on me. Or at least, that's my default assumption and the pick-up line that led to my triad with Rhea and Leo.

gallery.altText: An illustration of three people sitting together.

thumbnail.altText: An illustration of three people sitting together.

items.title: Bulldogs get the points at Docklands amid big injury worries for Saints

children.content: The Western Bulldogs get the points at Docklands against St Kilda to finish round 15, as the Saints lose two key players to injury.

image.altText: A Western Bulldogs tall forward is surrounded by teammates congratulating him after a vital goal.

items.title: Sharks antagonist raises Roosters' ire as Tricolours run through the finish

children.content: The Roosters finish with a wet sail to beat the Sharks 27-8 as Mark Nawaqanitawase gets a double in a heated but one-sided battle with opposite number Ronaldo Mulitalo.

image.altText: Roosters wrestle with ROnaldo Mulitalo during an NRL game.

items.title: Is 'baby brain' real? A neuroscientist explains

children.content: The fog new parents feel is real, but it seems to be driven less by motherhood rewiring the brain than by exhaustion, overload and the relentless demands of caring for a tiny human.

image.altText: A close up photo of a woman hugging and kissing a baby in a navy striped top

items.title: Family concerned over disappearance of 47-year-old Victorian man

children.content: The family of Horsham man Neville Nicholson is concerned for his safety and is desperate for his safe return.

image.altText: neville and family

items.title: Quick hits: Snakes at Germany's training and a player nicks a ref's watch

children.content: A Curaçao goalkeeper etches out a piece of World Cup history, Germany faces some slippery training pitch invaders and a referee's watch is briefly nicked by a player. Here are today's quick hits.

articles.title: Japan blitzes hapless Tunisia 4-0 as Curaçao makes World Cup history

children.content: Japan eases past Tunisia 4-0 in the 1,000th game at a men's World Cup after Curaçao goalkeeper Eloy Room produces an inspired performance to deliver his nation its first-ever point.

image.altText: Daichi Kamada holds his hand up to his face like a phone in celebration of a goal

articles.title: Is the most expensive World Cup in history worth the cost for travelling fans?

children.content: There has been endless talk about fans paying high prices for this World Cup. So is it worth it? And is it time to accept the people's game is no longer within everyone's reach?

image.altText: A composite image of several World Cup fans from different countries with face paint

items.title: Thunderbirds first team through to Super Netball Grand Final

children.content: The Adelaide Thunderbirds are through to their third Super Netball grand final in four years. Now they wait to see if they will face the Melbourne Vixens or West Coast Fever for a shot at the title.

image.altText: Players hug and smile as they celebrate in a huddle

items.title: Speedway plans spark protest

children.content: Residents of Beachmere in Moreton Bay say a proposed speedway track will “fundamentally change the character" of the small coastal town.

image.altText: Protesters stand behind a banner at Beachmere.

items.title: Family of shark attack victim says swift response 'saved' mother's life

children.content: A Sydney shark attack victim has been hailed "a survivor" by her family in a letter read out at a community swim event.

image.altText: Aerial drone vision of Coogee beach, locals crowd the shore ahead of a community swim-out.

gallery.altText: Surf lifesavers in uniform stand on the beach with boards while others swim in the water in the morning sun.

thumbnail.altText: Surf lifesavers in uniform stand on the beach with boards while others swim in the water in the morning sun.

items.title: Police speech stuns descendants gathered at site of outback mass killing

children.content: Ronnie's great-grandfather collected bones and teeth from an outback creek bed. Now police say they're 'not proud' of what occurred.

image.altText: a kimberly landscape aerial shows old buildings scattered in the scrub, with a winding river stretching through the frame
